events.text: The patient is a 38-year-old woman named Marlena Okonkwo. She has a family history significant for a maternal aunt diagnosed with ovarian cancer at age 52 and a maternal grandmother with breast cancer at age 60. She is of West African descent, nulliparous, premenopausal, and works as a high school biology teacher. She has no significant past medical history aside from mild intermittent asthma managed with an albuterol inhaler. She is a non-smoker and drinks alcohol occasionally.

At age 38 years, the patient had a diagnosis of Stage IIB (T2 N1 M0, AJCC 8th edition) invasive ductal carcinoma (IDC), grade 3, of the left breast, upper outer quadrant. ICD-O-3 site code C50.4 (Upper-outer quadrant of breast), histology code 8500/3 (Infiltrating duct carcinoma, NOS). Tumor size by imaging: 3.1 cm. Lymph node status: 1 clinically palpable ipsilateral axillary lymph node, biopsy-confirmed metastatic carcinoma. Receptor status: Estrogen receptor (ER) negative (Allred score 0/8), Progesterone receptor (PR) negative (Allred score 0/8), HER2 negative (IHC 1+, FISH not performed). Ki-67 proliferation index: 85%. Nuclear grade: 3. Lymphovascular invasion: present. Summary stage: Regional. Confirmation method: Positive histology via core needle biopsy. Laterality: Left. Tumor location: Upper outer quadrant. No evidence of distant metastasis on staging workup.

At age 38, the patient had a core needle biopsy pathology report from the left breast upper outer quadrant mass and left axillary lymph node fine-needle aspiration (FNA). The breast core biopsy showed invasive ductal carcinoma, grade 3 (Nottingham score 9/9: tubule formation 3, nuclear pleomorphism 3, mitotic count 3). Tumor cells were arranged in solid nests with central necrosis. Stromal tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes (TILs) were noted at 40%. Lymphovascular invasion was identified. ER: negative (0% staining, intensity 0). PR: negative (0% staining, intensity 0). HER2: 1+ by IHC (negative). Ki-67: 85%. The axillary lymph node FNA showed metastatic carcinoma consistent with primary breast origin. PD-L1 expression by combined positive score (CPS): 15 (22C3 assay, positive threshold >= 10).

At age 38, the patient had a diagnostic bilateral mammogram and ultrasound of the left breast and left axilla for staging purposes. Mammogram showed a 3.1 cm irregular, high-density mass in the left upper outer quadrant with spiculated margins and associated microcalcifications. No additional suspicious lesions in the right breast. Ultrasound confirmed a 3.1 x 2.8 x 2.5 cm hypoechoic, irregular mass at the 10 o'clock position, 3 cm from the nipple, with posterior acoustic enhancement. One enlarged left axillary lymph node measuring 2.0 cm with loss of fatty hilum was identified. BI-RADS category 5 (highly suggestive of malignancy). No skin thickening or nipple involvement. Cancer status: present. No distant metastatic sites identified.

At age 38, the patient had a staging CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with contrast, as well as a bone scan for distant metastasis evaluation. CT chest: No pulmonary nodules, pleural effusion, or mediastinal lymphadenopathy. Left axillary lymphadenopathy confirmed (largest node 2.0 cm). CT abdomen/pelvis: No liver lesions, adrenal masses, or retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy. No ascites. Bone scan: No scintigraphic evidence of osseous metastases. Overall impression: No evidence of distant metastatic disease. Staging consistent with locoregional disease, Stage IIB (T2 N1 M0). Cancer status: present, locoregional only.

At age 38 years, the patient had next-generation sequencing performed for triple-negative breast cancer based on a specimen obtained from the left breast core needle biopsy (primary tumor). Tissue-based comprehensive genomic profiling (CGP) panel (324-gene panel) showed the following findings: Actionable/clinically relevant alterations: TP53 p.R248W (pathogenic, variant allele frequency 48%), PIK3CA wild-type, BRCA1 p.E1250* (pathogenic germline-confirmed somatic loss, VAF 51%), PTEN loss (homozygous deletion), RB1 p.Q670* (pathogenic, VAF 39%). Co-mutations and other findings: MYC amplification (copy number 14), CCND1 wild-type, CDK4 wild-type, CDKN2A p.P114L (VAF 22%), RAD51C wild-type, PALB2 wild-type, CHEK2 wild-type, ATM wild-type, FANCA wild-type, BRIP1 wild-type, NBN wild-type. Fusions: none detected. Microsatellite status: microsatellite stable (MSS). Tumor mutational burden (TMB): 9.2 mutations/megabase (intermediate). PD-L1 CPS: 15 (confirmed on IHC). Homologous recombination deficiency (HRD) score: 54 (positive, threshold >= 42). Genomic loss of heterozygosity (gLOH): 22%. No NTRK, ALK, or RET fusions detected. Germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant confirmed on reflexed germline testing (c.3750G>A, p.E1250*, reported separately to genetic counseling).

At age 38, the patient had an oncology new patient consultation. She presented with a self-detected left breast mass noticed 6 weeks prior to presentation. Examination showed a firm, irregular, 3 cm palpable mass in the left upper outer quadrant, non-tender, with fixation to surrounding tissue. A single palpable left axillary lymph node approximately 2 cm was noted. No skin changes, nipple discharge, or contralateral findings. Performance status ECOG 0. Following tumor board discussion, the decision was made to pursue neoadjuvant systemic therapy given T2N1 disease and TNBC histology. Germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant confirmed; genetic counseling referral placed. Fertility preservation counseling offered and patient deferred (nulliparous, declined oocyte cryopreservation due to time constraints). Plan: neoadjuvant pembrolizumab 200 mg IV every 3 weeks plus dose-dense doxorubicin 60 mg/m2 and cyclophosphamide 600 mg/m2 every 2 weeks (ddAC) x 4 cycles, followed by paclitaxel 80 mg/m2 weekly x 12 weeks with concurrent pembrolizumab every 3 weeks, then definitive surgery. Cancer present. No response assessment yet (pre-treatment baseline).

At age 38 years, the patient received neoadjuvant-intent pembrolizumab 200 mg IV on day 1 of each 21-day cycle, concurrently with dose-dense doxorubicin (Adriamycin) 60 mg/m2 IV and cyclophosphamide 600 mg/m2 IV every 14 days (ddAC), for 4 cycles. Pegfilgrastim 6 mg SC was administered on day 2 of each ddAC cycle for primary prophylaxis of febrile neutropenia. Pembrolizumab cycle 1 day 1 administered concurrently with ddAC cycle 1. Total duration of ddAC phase: approximately 8 weeks. This was administered as part of the KEYNOTE-522-inspired neoadjuvant regimen for stage IIB TNBC with PD-L1 CPS >= 10.

At age 38 (approximately 5 weeks into neoadjuvant therapy, during ddAC cycle 3), the patient developed an immune-related adverse event (irAE): grade 2 immune-mediated hepatitis. Liver function tests showed ALT 186 U/L (4.2x ULN), AST 152 U/L (3.8x ULN), total bilirubin 1.1 mg/dL (within normal limits), alkaline phosphatase mildly elevated at 98 U/L. The patient was asymptomatic. Pembrolizumab was held. Work-up included hepatitis B and C serologies (negative), ANA (1:80 speckled pattern), anti-smooth muscle antibody (negative), ceruloplasmin (normal). Liver ultrasound showed no ductal dilation or masses. The patient was started on prednisone 1 mg/kg/day (60 mg/day). ddAC cycle 3 was administered on schedule as the hepatitis was attributed to pembrolizumab. LFTs normalized within 3 weeks. Prednisone tapered over 4 weeks. Pembrolizumab was resumed after confirmed LFT normalization.

At age 38 (during ddAC phase, cycle 2), the patient developed grade 2 chemotherapy-induced nausea and vomiting (CINV) despite scheduled ondansetron, dexamethasone, and aprepitant premedication. She required an additional rescue lorazepam dose and was seen in the infusion clinic for IV hydration on one occasion. No hospitalization required. Anti-emetic regimen was optimized for subsequent cycles with the addition of olanzapine 10 mg on days 1-4. Additionally, the patient experienced grade 1 alopecia beginning after cycle 1, which progressed to grade 2 complete alopecia by cycle 2 as expected with doxorubicin-based therapy.

At age 38, the patient had a mid-treatment response assessment MRI of the left breast and axilla after completion of ddAC x 4 cycles (prior to initiation of weekly paclitaxel). Dynamic contrast-enhanced (DCE) MRI showed the primary left breast mass had decreased from 3.1 cm to 1.8 cm (42% reduction in longest dimension). The previously identified left axillary lymph node had decreased in size from 2.0 cm to 1.0 cm with partial restoration of fatty hilum. No new suspicious lesions identified in either breast. No skin thickening. Impression: Partial response to neoadjuvant therapy. Residual tumor with radiographic partial response (per RECIST 1.1 criteria, partial response). Cancer status: present, responding. Recommendation: proceed with planned weekly paclitaxel plus pembrolizumab phase.

At age 38 years, the patient received the second phase of neoadjuvant therapy: paclitaxel 80 mg/m2 IV weekly for 12 weeks (administered days 1, 8, 15 of each 21-day cycle, for 4 cycles), with concurrent pembrolizumab 200 mg IV on day 1 of each 21-day cycle. Total of 12 paclitaxel infusions administered. This phase followed completion of ddAC x 4 cycles. Pembrolizumab was resumed after confirmed LFT normalization following prior irAE. Premedication with diphenhydramine, famotidine, and dexamethasone administered prior to each paclitaxel infusion.

At age 38 (during weekly paclitaxel phase, approximately week 8 of 12), the patient developed grade 2 peripheral sensory neuropathy affecting the bilateral hands and feet with numbness and tingling, impairing fine motor tasks (difficulty buttoning clothes, using a keyboard). Paclitaxel dose was reduced by 20% (to 64 mg/m2) for the remaining 4 weekly doses. The patient was counseled on neuropathy precautions. Duloxetine 30 mg daily was initiated for symptomatic management, titrated to 60 mg daily. No dose discontinuation required. Grade improved to grade 1 by the time of surgical planning.

At age 38, the patient had an oncology follow-up visit at completion of the full neoadjuvant regimen (ddAC x 4 cycles + weekly paclitaxel x 12 weeks + concurrent pembrolizumab x 8 cycles). Physical examination showed a residual palpable left breast mass estimated at approximately 1.5 cm, slightly firm. Left axillary lymph node was no longer palpable. Performance status ECOG 0-1 (mild fatigue, grade 1 neuropathy). The patient was referred for surgical planning. Plan: proceed to definitive surgery -- left lumpectomy with sentinel lymph node biopsy vs. axillary lymph node dissection, based on intraoperative findings. Pembrolizumab was held in the perioperative period per institutional protocol. Cancer present with partial response to neoadjuvant therapy.

At age 38 years, the patient underwent a left partial mastectomy (lumpectomy) with wire localization of the residual tumor bed plus sentinel lymph node biopsy (SLNB) under general anesthesia. Intraoperative findings: residual firm mass in the left upper outer quadrant, approximately 1.5 cm by palpation. Three sentinel lymph nodes were identified and excised (blue dye and radiocolloid technique). Intraoperative frozen section of sentinel lymph node 1 showed metastatic carcinoma (micrometastasis, 0.4 mm focus). Given micrometastatic involvement, axillary lymph node dissection (ALND) was performed (levels I and II). ALND yielded 14 additional lymph nodes. Surgical margins were obtained in all six directions. Operative time: 3 hours 10 minutes. Estimated blood loss: 80 mL. No intraoperative complications. Patient recovered uneventfully and was discharged on postoperative day 1.

At age 38, the patient had a definitive surgical pathology report from left lumpectomy and axillary lymph node dissection. Gross specimen: Lumpectomy specimen 4.5 x 3.8 x 2.2 cm, with clip at tumor bed. Microscopic findings: Residual invasive ductal carcinoma, grade 3, measuring 1.4 cm in greatest dimension. Extensive treatment effect noted with large areas of fibrosis, ghost cell formations, and foamy macrophage infiltration (approximately 55% treatment-related fibrosis). Residual viable tumor cells comprising approximately 45% of the original tumor bed area. Lymphovascular invasion not identified in residual tumor. Margins: all margins negative (closest margin: posterior, 3 mm). Lymph nodes: 1 of 17 nodes positive (1 sentinel node with micrometastasis 0.4 mm, isolated tumor cell cluster; 0 of 14 ALND nodes positive, 0 of 2 remaining sentinel nodes positive). ypT1c ypN0(i+) -- residual pathologic stage ypT1c ypN0(i+) M0. Residual Cancer Burden (RCB) class: RCB-II (intermediate residual disease). Miller-Payne grade: 3 (30-90% reduction in tumor cellularity). ER, PR, HER2 on residual tumor: negative (triple-negative confirmed). Ki-67 on residual tumor: 60% (down from 85% at diagnosis). Stromal TILs in residual tumor: 10% (decreased from 40% at diagnosis).

At age 38 years, the patient received adjuvant whole breast radiation therapy (WBRT) to the left breast following lumpectomy, with a sequential boost to the tumor bed. Total dose: 50 Gy in 25 fractions to the whole left breast (2 Gy/fraction, 5 days/week over 5 weeks), followed by a sequential electron boost of 10 Gy in 5 fractions to the lumpectomy cavity (total tumor bed dose 60 Gy). Concurrent regional nodal irradiation (RNI) was administered to the left supraclavicular, infraclavicular, and axillary (levels I-III) nodal regions given node-positive disease, to a dose of 50 Gy in 25 fractions. Treatment planning: 3D conformal with tangential fields and direct supraclavicular field. Normal tissue constraints met for lung (V20 < 15%) and heart (mean dose < 2 Gy, left-sided). No acute grade 3+ toxicity. Grade 2 radiation dermatitis developed in weeks 4-5, managed with topical silver sulfadiazine and moisture barrier cream.

At age 38 (during adjuvant radiation therapy, weeks 4-5 of WBRT), the patient developed grade 2 radiation dermatitis of the left breast with erythema, moist desquamation in the inframammary fold, and moderate pain (pain score 5/10). Managed with topical silver sulfadiazine cream twice daily, moisture barrier ointment, and oral ibuprofen 400 mg TID as needed. No treatment interruption required. Skin toxicity resolved to grade 1 by 3 weeks post-radiation completion. Additionally, mild fatigue (grade 1-2) persisted throughout radiation, attributed to cumulative treatment fatigue. The patient continued working part-time during radiation.

At age 38 years, the patient received adjuvant capecitabine 1000 mg/m2 orally twice daily on days 1-14 of each 21-day cycle for 6 cycles (total approximately 18 weeks of capecitabine therapy), in accordance with the CREATE-X trial data for TNBC patients with residual disease after neoadjuvant chemotherapy. Additionally, adjuvant pembrolizumab 200 mg IV every 3 weeks was resumed and continued for a planned total of 1 year (17 cycles total including neoadjuvant doses; approximately 9 additional cycles in the adjuvant setting). Capecitabine and pembrolizumab were initiated approximately 3-4 weeks after completion of radiation therapy. The patient was counseled on hand-foot syndrome prevention with urea cream and dose modification criteria.

At age 38 (approximately 6 weeks into adjuvant capecitabine, capecitabine cycle 2), the patient developed grade 2 palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia (hand-foot syndrome, HFS) with painful erythema, swelling, and tenderness of the bilateral palms and soles, interfering with daily activities (difficulty standing for prolonged periods in the classroom). Capecitabine was dose-reduced by 25% (to 750 mg/m2 BID) per standard dose modification guidelines. Topical urea 20% cream was applied twice daily to affected areas; pyridoxine 200 mg daily was added. HFS improved to grade 1 within 2 weeks. Capecitabine was continued at the reduced dose for cycles 3-6 without further dose modifications. No additional capecitabine dose reductions required.

At age 38-39, the patient had an oncology follow-up visit at completion of adjuvant capecitabine (6 cycles complete). She tolerated the regimen reasonably well with capecitabine dose reduction for HFS as noted. Pembrolizumab maintenance continued (currently on cycle 14 total). Physical examination: well-appearing, ECOG 0. Left breast: no palpable mass, expected post-lumpectomy changes. No axillary adenopathy bilaterally. Pertinent labs: CBC within normal limits, LFTs normal, TSH 6.8 mIU/L (mildly elevated, upper limit normal 4.5). Free T4 0.82 ng/dL (low). Assessment: likely pembrolizumab-associated primary hypothyroidism (grade 1-2 irAE). Levothyroxine 50 mcg daily initiated; pembrolizumab continued without interruption. TSH normalized at subsequent visit. Patient counseled regarding ongoing germline BRCA1 mutation implications for risk-reducing surgery (bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy consideration; deferred to age 40). Cancer status: no evidence of disease (NED).

At age 39, the patient had a surveillance bilateral breast MRI (right breast full, left breast post-lumpectomy) approximately 12 months after surgery. Right breast: No suspicious enhancement, no masses, no lymphadenopathy. Left breast: Post-surgical and post-radiation changes in the left upper outer quadrant with expected architectural distortion, fat necrosis, and clip artifact. No new suspicious enhancement. No enlarged axillary, internal mammary, or supraclavicular lymph nodes. Background parenchymal enhancement: moderate. Impression: No evidence of local or regional recurrence. No new suspicious findings. Cancer status: no evidence of disease.

At age 39, the patient completed her planned 1 year (17 total cycles) of pembrolizumab. Final pembrolizumab dose administered. She reported ongoing grade 1 peripheral neuropathy (feet only, stable, not worsening). Hypothyroidism well-controlled on levothyroxine 50 mcg. She returned to full-time teaching. She underwent genetic counseling follow-up; her two sisters were referred for BRCA1 testing. Patient declined risk-reducing bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy (RRSO) at this time citing desire to discuss with partner. She will continue annual breast MRI surveillance and semi-annual oncology visits. Plan: surveillance with clinical exams every 6 months, annual bilateral breast MRI, annual mammogram (contralateral breast), CA 27-29 not routinely monitored per guidelines. No evidence of disease at this visit.

At age 39 (approximately 18 months post-surgery), the patient had annual surveillance imaging: diagnostic mammogram of the right breast and left breast (post-lumpectomy views) plus bilateral breast MRI. Mammogram: Right breast negative. Left breast: stable post-surgical changes, clip artifact at lumpectomy site, no new calcifications. MRI: Right breast no suspicious lesions. Left breast: expected post-treatment changes, no new enhancement. Left axilla: no lymphadenopathy. Liver: not evaluated on breast MRI. Cancer status: no evidence of disease. Note: patient also reported new right-sided rib pain rated 3/10, described as constant and non-pleuritic. Physical exam was unremarkable. Given BRCA1 germline mutation and residual disease at surgery (RCB-II), further evaluation ordered.

At age 39 (18 months post-surgery, prompted by new rib pain), the patient had a whole-body bone scan and CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast for evaluation of possible distant recurrence. Bone scan: Multiple foci of increased radiotracer uptake identified: right 5th rib (posterior), T10 vertebral body, right iliac wing. Findings suspicious for osseous metastases. CT chest: No pulmonary parenchymal nodules or masses. Right 5th rib lesion: lytic-appearing, 1.5 cm, with cortical disruption. CT abdomen: Liver shows two hypoattenuating lesions, right lobe, measuring 1.2 cm and 0.8 cm. No adenopathy above diaphragm. CT pelvis: Right iliac wing lesion 1.8 cm, lytic, with subtle cortical disruption. Impression: Highly suspicious for distant metastatic disease involving the osseous skeleton and liver. Cancer status: present, likely metastatic. Recommend biopsy confirmation.

At age 39, the patient underwent CT-guided core needle biopsy of the right iliac wing lesion for pathologic confirmation of suspected distant recurrence. Pathology: Metastatic carcinoma, consistent with breast primary. Histology: poorly differentiated carcinoma with solid architecture, high nuclear grade. Immunohistochemistry: ER negative (0%), PR negative (0%), HER2 negative (IHC 1+, not amplified), CK7 positive, CK20 negative, GATA3 positive, mammaglobin focally positive, TTF-1 negative. Ki-67: 72%. Diagnosis: Metastatic triple-negative breast carcinoma to the right iliac wing. PD-L1 CPS on metastatic specimen: 8 (below threshold of 10 for first-line pembrolizumab in metastatic setting). Liquid biopsy (circulating tumor DNA, ctDNA) from concurrent blood draw showed BRCA1 p.E1250* (VAF 4.2%), TP53 p.R248W (VAF 3.8%), PTEN loss (detected), new PIK3CA p.H1047R (VAF 1.1%, acquired mutation not present on primary tumor NGS), MYC amplification detected.

At age 39 years, the patient had next-generation sequencing performed on the metastatic iliac wing biopsy specimen (bone metastasis, right iliac wing). Tissue-based comprehensive genomic profiling (CGP) showed: TP53 p.R248W (pathogenic, VAF 45%), BRCA1 p.E1250* (pathogenic, VAF 49%), PTEN loss (homozygous deletion, confirmed), PIK3CA p.H1047R (pathogenic, acquired, VAF 18% -- not present on primary tumor), RB1 p.Q670* (pathogenic, VAF 38%), MYC amplification (copy number 18, increased from 14 on primary), CDKN2A homozygous deletion (new finding, not detected at primary), CDK4 amplification (copy number 8, new finding -- co-occurring with CDKN2A loss, consistent with CDK4/6 pathway activation). ESR1 wild-type. FGFR2 wild-type. No NTRK fusions. No ALK fusions. Microsatellite status: MSS. TMB: 11.4 mutations/megabase (intermediate-high). HRD score: 61 (positive, increased from 54 at primary). PD-L1 CPS: 8 on metastatic specimen (decreased from 15 on primary). The acquired PIK3CA p.H1047R mutation and CDK4 amplification represent new targetable alterations in the metastatic setting, suggesting clonal evolution during treatment.

At age 39, the patient had an oncology visit for counseling and treatment planning following confirmed distant metastatic TNBC recurrence (bone and liver). ECOG performance status 1 (rib pain managed with scheduled acetaminophen and ibuprofen, now additionally prescribed opioid for breakthrough pain control; radiation oncology consulted for palliative radiation to symptomatic rib lesion). Disease-free interval from surgery to metastatic recurrence: approximately 18 months. The case was reviewed at multidisciplinary tumor board. Key considerations: (1) prior pembrolizumab exposure (completed 17 cycles adjuvant, now PD-L1 CPS 8 on metastatic specimen, immune exhaustion possible); (2) germline BRCA1 mutation -- PARP inhibitor (olaparib or talazoparib) indicated; (3) acquired PIK3CA p.H1047R -- potential alpelisib consideration in future; (4) HRD positive. Treatment plan for first-line metastatic: olaparib 300 mg BID as monotherapy given germline BRCA1 mutation and prior platinum-free interval, per OlympiAD trial data. Palliative radiation for right 5th rib. Referral to palliative care. Cancer present, metastatic.

At age 39 years, the patient received palliative radiation therapy to the right 5th posterior rib for pain control. Dose: 20 Gy in 5 fractions (4 Gy/fraction, delivered over 5 days) using 3D conformal planning with opposed lateral fields. Treatment intent: palliative (pain control, fracture prevention). The patient tolerated palliative radiation well with no acute grade 3+ toxicities. Pain at the rib site improved from 5/10 to 1/10 within 2 weeks of treatment completion. No radiation skin reactions. Concurrent olaparib was held for 1 week during radiation per institutional protocol and resumed on day 8.

At age 39 years, the patient initiated first-line treatment for metastatic triple-negative breast cancer with germline BRCA1 mutation: olaparib (Lynparza) 300 mg orally twice daily (continuous dosing). This was selected based on the OlympiAD trial evidence for germline BRCA1/2-mutated HER2-negative metastatic breast cancer. Concurrent bone-modifying agent: zoledronic acid 4 mg IV every 4 weeks initiated for bone metastases (along with calcium 1200 mg/day and vitamin D 800 IU/day supplementation, with baseline dental evaluation completed). Olaparib started after completion of palliative rib radiation and after a 48-hour washout.

At age 39 (approximately 3 months after initiation of olaparib for metastatic disease), the patient had restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast plus bone scan for response assessment. CT abdomen: Right lobe hepatic lesions stable to slightly decreased -- the 1.2 cm lesion now measures 0.9 cm and the 0.8 cm lesion is now 0.6 cm. No new liver lesions. CT chest: No new pulmonary lesions. Right 5th rib: post-radiation changes, no new fracture. Bone scan: Previously identified foci of uptake at T10 and right iliac wing are stable. No new sites of radiotracer uptake. No new bone lesions on CT. Impression: Partial response to olaparib therapy in the liver; stable osseous disease. Overall assessment: partial response per RECIST 1.1 (combined liver lesion reduction > 30%). Cancer status: present, responding. Plan: continue olaparib at current dose, continue zoledronic acid, reassess in 3 months.

At age 39-40, the patient had an oncology follow-up visit at 6 months of olaparib therapy for metastatic TNBC. She reported grade 1 fatigue and grade 1 nausea (managed with ondansetron PRN), tolerated well overall. Complete blood count showed mild anemia: hemoglobin 10.2 g/dL (grade 1, baseline 12.8 g/dL), MCV 98 fL (macrocytic pattern consistent with PARP inhibitor effect). Reticulocyte count low-normal. No dose reduction required (threshold for reduction is hemoglobin < 8 g/dL or symptomatic). Iron studies: ferritin 68 ng/mL, transferrin saturation 24% (no iron deficiency). The patient reported that her sister tested positive for BRCA1 and was referred for risk-reduction counseling. Performance status ECOG 1. No new symptoms. She decided to proceed with bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy for ovarian cancer risk reduction given BRCA1 mutation (scheduled with gynecologic oncology). Cancer present (known metastatic disease), responding to olaparib.

documents.text:
DYNAMIC CONTRAST-ENHANCED MRI OF THE BILATERAL BREASTS AND LEFT AXILLA
MID-TREATMENT RESPONSE ASSESSMENT

CLINICAL INDICATION:
38-year-old premenopausal woman with biopsy-proven stage IIB (T2N1M0) triple-negative invasive ductal carcinoma of the left breast (upper outer quadrant), grade 3, ER/PR/HER2 negative, Ki-67 85%, PD-L1 CPS 15. Germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant confirmed. Patient has completed 4 cycles of dose-dense doxorubicin and cyclophosphamide (ddAC) with concurrent pembrolizumab as neoadjuvant therapy. MRI obtained for mid-treatment response assessment prior to initiation of second phase (weekly paclitaxel with pembrolizumab).

COMPARISON:
Prior breast ultrasound and mammogram at time of initial staging workup.

TECHNIQUE:
Dynamic contrast-enhanced (DCE) MRI of the bilateral breasts performed at 3.0 Tesla using a dedicated bilateral breast coil. Sequences obtained: axial T1-weighted pre-contrast, axial T2-weighted fat-suppressed, axial diffusion-weighted imaging (DWI) with ADC mapping (b = 0, 800 s/mm2), and dynamic axial T1-weighted fat-suppressed post-contrast series (5 phases at 60-second intervals following IV gadolinium administration -- 0.1 mmol/kg gadobutrol). Maximum intensity projection (MIP) reconstructions, subtraction images, and kinetic curve analysis performed. Axial and sagittal reformats provided.

FINDINGS:

LEFT BREAST:
The primary lesion in the left upper outer quadrant (10 o'clock position, approximately 3 cm from the nipple) demonstrates a markedly irregular, spiculated mass with heterogeneous internal enhancement. On this study the mass measures approximately 1.8 cm in greatest dimension (previously 3.1 cm on ultrasound at baseline), representing a substantial reduction in size. The margins remain somewhat irregular with a small area of satellite enhancement immediately adjacent to the dominant mass that may represent residual satellite tumor or reactive perilesional stroma; this is not clearly separable from the primary lesion. The biopsy clip is well-visualized within the lesion.

On T2-weighted sequences, there is residual heterogeneous signal within the mass with some areas of high T2 signal, which may reflect treatment-related edema or necrosis mixed with residual viable tumor. ADC values within the residual mass range from 0.85 to 1.10 x 10^-3 mm2/s (previously low ADC consistent with high cellularity at diagnosis); the increase in ADC is consistent with decreased tumor cellularity in treatment-responsive areas.

Kinetic enhancement pattern: The majority of the residual mass demonstrates type II (plateau) kinetics with a smaller region of type III (washout) kinetics at the periphery, suggestive of residual viable tumor in the peripheral component.

No new suspicious enhancing lesions identified elsewhere in the left breast. Skin is not thickened. No nipple-areolar complex involvement. No abnormal pectoralis muscle enhancement.

LEFT AXILLA:
The previously identified enlarged left axillary lymph node now measures approximately 1.0 cm in short axis (previously 2.0 cm at baseline). There is partial restoration of the fatty hilum, now more evident than at baseline when the node showed near-complete loss of fatty hilum. No additional enlarged or morphologically suspicious axillary nodes are identified. The lower axillary region appears more favorable than at baseline.

RIGHT BREAST:
No suspicious enhancing lesion. No masses, architectural distortion, or pathologic regional lymphadenopathy. Background parenchymal enhancement is mild to moderate, symmetric. No nipple discharge enhancement. No axillary adenopathy on the right.

BILATERAL FINDINGS:
No chest wall involvement. No internal mammary or supraclavicular nodal enlargement. No skin thickening or inflammatory changes bilaterally. No evidence of intramammary edema beyond expected perilesional changes on the left.

IMPRESSION:

1. Left breast primary lesion: Significant reduction in size of the dominant upper outer quadrant mass, now measuring approximately 1.8 cm compared to 3.1 cm at initial imaging. The mass is smaller and demonstrates increased ADC values consistent with treatment effect, though residual heterogeneous enhancement with washout kinetics at the lesion periphery is concerning for viable residual tumor. The biopsy clip remains in situ.

2. Left axillary lymphadenopathy: Substantial decrease in the size of the dominant axillary lymph node with partial recovery of the fatty hilum, suggesting favorable nodal response to therapy. No additional suspicious nodes.

3. Right breast and bilateral remainder: No suspicious findings.

4. Overall, these findings are consistent with a meaningful tumor response to neoadjuvant therapy following 4 cycles of ddAC with concurrent pembrolizumab, with residual disease present at both the primary site and, to a lesser degree, at the regional axillary level. Complete pathologic response cannot be determined radiographically.

MEDICAL ONCOLOGY -- FOLLOW-UP VISIT
Completion of Neoadjuvant Therapy / Pre-Surgical Assessment

ATTENDING: [Attending Oncologist]
REFERRING PROVIDER: [Surgical Oncology]

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT
Ms. Okonkwo is a 38-year-old woman with stage IIB (T2N1M0) left breast triple-negative invasive ductal carcinoma, grade 3, germline BRCA1 p.E1250* mutation carrier, presenting today for end-of-neoadjuvant-therapy assessment following completion of the full KEYNOTE-522 inspired regimen. She is here for physical exam, toxicity review, pre-surgical planning, and discussion of next steps.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS
Ms. Okonkwo originally presented approximately 5 months ago with a self-detected left breast mass. Workup confirmed a 3.1 cm left upper outer quadrant mass (10 o'clock position, 3 cm from nipple) with a single enlarged left axillary lymph node (2.0 cm). Core biopsy confirmed grade 3 invasive ductal carcinoma: ER 0%, PR 0%, HER2 1+ by IHC (triple-negative). Ki-67 was strikingly high at 85%. PD-L1 CPS 15 (22C3). TILs 40%. Germline testing reflexed from tumor NGS confirmed a pathogenic BRCA1 variant (c.3750G>A, p.E1250*). Staging CT and bone scan showed no distant metastatic disease. She was staged cT2N1M0, Stage IIB.

She was enrolled on a neoadjuvant regimen consisting of pembrolizumab 200 mg IV q3weeks plus dose-dense doxorubicin/cyclophosphamide (ddAC) q2 weeks x 4 cycles, followed by weekly paclitaxel 80 mg/m2 x 12 weeks with concurrent pembro q3 weeks. Pegfilgrastim was administered on day 2 of each ddAC cycle for primary febrile neutropenia prophylaxis.

Her course was notable for the following:

1. Grade 2 immune-mediated hepatitis, occurring during ddAC cycle 3 (approximately 5 weeks into therapy). ALT peaked at 186 U/L (~4x ULN), AST 152 U/L. She was asymptomatic. Pembrolizumab was held; prednisone 1 mg/kg/day was started. LFTs normalized within 3 weeks. Pembro was resumed after confirmed normalization. Infectious and autoimmune hepatitis workup was negative (HBV/HCV negative, ASMA negative, ANA 1:80 speckled -- likely low-titer nonspecific). ddAC cycles were administered on schedule throughout.

COMMENT

The morphologic and immunophenotypic findings are consistent with a poorly differentiated invasive ductal carcinoma (invasive carcinoma of no special type) of the left breast, upper outer quadrant. The triple-negative receptor phenotype (ER-/PR-/HER2-), high Ki-67 proliferative index, and elevated stromal TILs at 40% are in keeping with the immunomodulatory subtype of TNBC. The elevated PD-L1 CPS of 15 by the 22C3 assay meets the threshold for PD-L1 positivity in breast cancer. Lymphovascular invasion is present. Left axillary lymph node FNA is positive for metastatic carcinoma.

These findings have been discussed with the referring clinical team. Pathologic staging contribution: pT2 (3.1 cm by imaging correlate), cN1 (biopsy-confirmed axillary node). Complete pathologic staging deferred to resection specimen.

CLINICAL HISTORY
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The patient is a 39-year-old woman with a prior history of left breast invasive ductal carcinoma, grade 3, ER/PR/HER2-negative (triple-negative), initially Stage IIB (pT2 pN1a), diagnosed approximately 18 months prior. She was treated with neoadjuvant pembro plus dose-dense Adriamycin/cyclo followed by weekly Taxol/Keytruda, then underwent left partial mastectomy with sentinel lymph node biopsy and axillary lymph node dissection (ypT1c ypN0(i+), RCB-II). She now presents with new right-sided rib pain and was found to have multiple new lytic osseous lesions and hepatic lesions on cross-sectional imaging and bone scintigraphy. CT-guided core needle biopsy of the right iliac wing lytic lesion was performed for pathologic confirmation.

Germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant known (p.E1250*).

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
TYPE OF PROCEDURE
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

CT-guided core needle biopsy, right iliac wing (image-guided, interventional radiology). Multiple core passes performed under CT fluoroscopic guidance using a coaxial 11-gauge bone biopsy needle system. Four cores submitted in formalin.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
GROSS PATHOLOGY
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Specimen A: Received in formalin, labeled with the patient's name and "right iliac wing biopsy." The specimen consists of four cylindrical core biopsy fragments of gray-white to tan-gray tissue with admixed hemorrhage and focal gritty calcified bone trabeculae. Aggregate core length: approximately 1.5 cm total; individual core lengths range from 0.2 to 0.5 cm. Cores are submitted entirely in two cassettes (A1 and A2), with representative cores decalcified prior to sectioning.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
MICROSCOPIC FINDINGS
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Sections demonstrate fragments of bone and marrow space extensively infiltrated by sheets and irregular nests of neoplastic epithelial cells. The neoplastic cells are large with markedly enlarged, pleomorphic nuclei, prominent macronucleoli, and scant to moderate amounts of pale to eosinophilic cytoplasm. Nuclear-to-cytoplasmic ratios are high. Mitotic figures are readily identified (estimated >10/10 high-power fields), including atypical forms. Tumor cells are arranged predominantly in solid sheets and irregular trabeculae with focal central comedonecrosis. No glandular/tubular differentiation is identified.

The background bone marrow shows replacement of normal hematopoietic elements by tumor, with associated desmoplastic stroma and scattered reactive osteoclast-like giant cells at the periphery of bony trabeculae. Osteolysis is evident with thinning and focal disruption of residual trabeculae. There is no geographic bone necrosis to suggest prior treatment effect in this specimen.

No lymphovascular invasion is definitively identified within the biopsy cores. No perineural invasion seen.

The histomorphology is that of a poorly differentiated carcinoma with high nuclear grade (Grade 3), consistent with the patient's known triple-negative breast primary.

DIAGNOSIS

Specimen A -- Right iliac wing, CT-guided core needle biopsy:

  METASTATIC CARCINOMA, CONSISTENT WITH BREAST PRIMARY
  -- Poorly differentiated (Grade 3) carcinoma with solid architecture and comedonecrosis
  -- Immunophenotype: ER negative, PR negative, HER2 negative (IHC 1+), CK7 positive, GATA3 positive, mammaglobin focally positive, TTF-1 negative, CK20 negative -- profile consistent with and supportive of metastatic triple-negative breast carcinoma

Comment:
The morphologic and immunohistochemical findings are consistent with metastatic carcinoma of breast origin in the clinical context of this patient's documented history of high-grade, triple-negative invasive ductal carcinoma of the left breast. The immunoprofile (CK7+, GATA3+, mammaglobin focally+, CK20-, TTF-1-, PAX8-, CDX2-) strongly supports a breast primary and effectively excludes lung, gynecologic, gastrointestinal, and urothelial primaries. The tumor retains the triple-negative receptor profile seen at initial diagnosis (ER-/PR-/HER2- by IHC), with a Ki-67 of ~72% (reduced from 85% at diagnosis, though this comparison is limited by differences in specimen type and processing). The PD-L1 CPS of 8 on this specimen is noted to be lower than the CPS of 15 documented at initial core needle biopsy, a finding that has been reported in the context of prior anti-PD-1 therapy and warrants clinical correlation.

METHODOLOGY
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Platform: Hybrid-capture next-generation sequencing, 324-gene comprehensive genomic profiling (CGP) panel. Analysis includes coding exons of all 324 genes plus selected intronic and intergenic regions for fusion and copy number detection. DNA was extracted from FFPE tissue following macrodissection to enrich for tumor content. Libraries were prepared using standard adaptor ligation. Sequencing performed on Illumina platform with mean exon coverage >500x. Bioinformatic analysis performed using validated somatic variant calling pipelines. Germline vs. somatic status assessed by comparison with matched non-neoplastic (buffy coat) DNA where available; germline variants confirmed by reflexed germline testing. Copy number variants assessed by comparative analysis versus internal reference diploid samples. Microsatellite instability (MSI) status assessed by analysis of >200 microsatellite loci. Tumor mutational burden (TMB) calculated as number of somatic mutations per megabase of sequenced coding genome. Homologous recombination deficiency (HRD) score and genomic loss of heterozygosity (gLOH) calculated using validated computational algorithm.

Limitation: This assay detects alterations in tumor-derived DNA only. Some classes of genomic alterations (e.g., epigenetic silencing, large structural rearrangements outside captured regions) may not be detected. Variants of unknown significance (VUS) not listed here do not exclude their potential future clinical relevance.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

This metastatic bone biopsy specimen demonstrates a genomic landscape consistent with clonal evolution from the previously profiled primary triple-negative breast carcinoma, with the acquisition of several new somatic alterations in the metastatic setting. The following clinically relevant findings are highlighted:

--- TIER I: STRONG CLINICAL SIGNIFICANCE ---

[1] BRCA1 p.E1250* (germline-confirmed, biallelic inactivation via somatic LOH)
This pathogenic germline nonsense variant results in premature truncation of the BRCA1 protein, abrogating its homologous recombination DNA repair function. Biallelic inactivation (confirmed by somatic loss of heterozygosity at the second allele) renders tumor cells dependent on alternative DNA repair pathways. This variant was present in the primary tumor and is retained in the metastasis, confirming its role as a founding driver alteration. This finding is of strong direct therapeutic relevance: BRCA1/2 germline-mutated, HER2-negative metastatic breast cancer is a validated indication for PARP inhibitor therapy (olaparib, talazoparib; Tier I evidence, FDA-approved, OlympiAD and EMBRACA trials). Elevated HRD score (61) and high gLOH (28%) further support a robust homologous recombination-deficient tumor phenotype. This finding was the basis for initiation of olaparib 300 mg BID as noted in clinical records.

[2] PIK3CA p.H1047R (ACQUIRED somatic, VAF 18%)
This is an activating missense mutation in exon 20 (helical domain) of PIK3CA, one of the two most common hotspot mutations in PI3K-alpha (alongside p.E545K). It results in constitutive activation of PI3K signaling and downstream AKT/mTOR pathway engagement. This variant was not detected on primary tumor sequencing (performed at diagnosis) and represents a treatment-emergent acquired somatic alteration, consistent with clonal evolution during neoadjuvant and adjuvant systemic therapy. In the context of the co-existing PTEN homozygous deletion, PIK3CA p.H1047R further amplifies PI3K pathway activation. Notably, PIK3CA p.H1047R is a validated actionable target in hormone receptor-positive metastatic breast cancer (alpelisib + fulvestrant; SOLAR-1 trial). Its actionability in the TNBC setting remains investigational; however, this mutation would qualify the patient for PI3K-targeted clinical trial enrollment and may be of future clinical relevance if disease progresses on current PARP inhibitor therapy.

--- TIER II: POTENTIAL CLINICAL SIGNIFICANCE ---

[3] CDK4 Amplification (copy number 8, ACQUIRED)
High-level CDK4 amplification is identified as a new finding in the metastatic specimen, absent from the primary tumor. CDK4 amplification co-occurring with CDKN2A homozygous deletion (see below) results in unchecked CDK4/6 kinase activity, bypassing cell cycle checkpoints at the G1/S transition. While CDK4/6 inhibitors (palbociclib, ribociclib, abemaciclib) are primarily approved in HR+ breast cancer, CDK4 amplification represents a biologically plausible acquired mechanism of resistance to prior therapies and is a potential future therapeutic target. CDK4 amplification in TNBC is an area of active clinical investigation.

[4] CDKN2A Homozygous Deletion (ACQUIRED)
CDKN2A (encoding p16/INK4A and p14/ARF) is homozygously deleted in this metastatic specimen, representing a new alteration not present at primary diagnosis (previously heterozygous point mutation p.P114L). Homozygous loss of CDKN2A eliminates the primary negative regulator of CDK4/6, synergizing with CDK4 amplification to drive unrestrained cell cycle progression. The concurrent acquisition of CDK4 amplification and CDKN2A biallelic loss in the metastatic setting strongly suggests convergent selection for CDK4/6 pathway activation during disease evolution.

[5] PTEN Homozygous Deletion (present in primary, retained in metastasis)
Biallelic PTEN loss results in constitutive PI3K/AKT pathway activation independent of upstream signaling. In the context of acquired PIK3CA p.H1047R in the metastatic clone, PTEN loss further amplifies PI3K-AKT signaling. PTEN loss is additionally associated with reduced responsiveness to immune checkpoint inhibitors and may in part explain the decrease in PD-L1 CPS from 15 (primary tumor) to 8 (metastatic specimen).

[6] MYC High-Level Amplification (copy number 18, increased from 14 in primary)
Progressive increase in MYC amplification between primary and metastatic specimens is consistent with ongoing chromosomal instability and clonal evolution. MYC amplification drives global transcriptional reprogramming and is associated with aggressive disease biology and poor prognosis. MYC is not currently a direct pharmacologic target, though MYC-driven tumors may exhibit specific synthetic lethal dependencies.

--- TIER III: UNCERTAIN/ADDITIONAL SIGNIFICANCE ---

[7] TP53 p.R248W (present in primary, retained in metastasis; VAF stable at 45%)
The R248W mutation is one of the most common gain-of-function TP53 hotspot mutations. It acts through dominant-negative inhibition of wild-type p53 and acquisition of neomorphic oncogenic activities (enhanced invasion, altered gene expression programs). The stable VAF between primary and metastatic specimens is consistent with a clonal founder event. TP53 mutations remain without direct targeted therapy in clinical use.

[8] RB1 p.Q670* (present in primary, retained in metastasis; biallelic inactivation confirmed)
Biallelic RB1 inactivation (truncating mutation + hemizygous deletion of second allele) eliminates a critical tumor suppressor function at the G1/S checkpoint. RB1 loss may contribute to resistance to CDK4/6 inhibitors and immunotherapy. It is also associated with transition to an androgen receptor-expressing or neuroendocrine phenotype in some breast cancers, though this was not evaluated on the present specimen.

Comparison with prior comprehensive genomic profiling of the primary left breast tumor (core needle biopsy, performed at initial diagnosis) reveals the following key changes in the metastatic setting:

  Alterations RETAINED from primary:    TP53 p.R248W, BRCA1 p.E1250*, PTEN homozygous deletion, RB1 p.Q670*, MYC amplification
  Alterations with INCREASED magnitude: MYC amplification (CN 14 → 18), HRD score (54 → 61), gLOH (22% → 28%), TMB (9.2 → 11.4 mut/Mb)
  NEWLY ACQUIRED alterations:           PIK3CA p.H1047R (VAF 18%), CDK4 amplification (CN 8), CDKN2A homozygous deletion
  Alterations LOST or NOT DETECTED:     CDKN2A p.P114L (now superseded by homozygous deletion)

The overall picture is consistent with oligoclonal evolution under treatment selection pressure, with convergent activation of the CDK4/6 and PI3K pathways as newly acquired drivers in the metastatic clone.

MEDICAL ONCOLOGY -- FOLLOW-UP VISIT
Metastatic Breast Cancer -- Olaparib Cycle 9 (Approximately 6 Months on Therapy)

ATTENDING: [Attending Oncologist]
FELLOW/APP: [Fellow/APP]

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT
Ms. Okonkwo is a 39-year-old woman with metastatic triple-negative breast cancer (TNBC), germline BRCA1 mutation, presenting for routine follow-up on olaparib (Lynparza) therapy, approximately 6 months since initiation.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS
Ms. Okonkwo is a 39-year-old nulliparous premenopausal woman of West African descent with a history of stage IIB (T2N1M0) grade 3 triple-negative invasive ductal carcinoma of the left breast diagnosed approximately 18 months prior. She received neoadjuvant pembrolizumab plus dose-dense AC (ddAC x4 cycles) followed by weekly Taxol x12 weeks with concurrent pembro, then underwent left lumpectomy with SLNB/ALND (ypT1c ypN0(i+), RCB-II, Miller-Payne grade 3). She completed adjuvant whole-breast radiation with regional nodal irradiation, adjuvant capecitabine x6 cycles (dose-reduced for grade 2 hand-foot syndrome), and completed a total of 17 cycles of adjuvant pembrolizumab. Germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant (c.3750G>A, p.E1250*) was confirmed at diagnosis.

Approximately 18 months after surgery she developed new right-sided rib pain, and restaging workup revealed metastatic disease with right 5th rib/posterior, T10 vertebral body, and right iliac wing osseous involvement, as well as two right hepatic lobe lesions (measuring 1.2 cm and 0.8 cm on initial staging CT). CT-guided biopsy of the right iliac wing confirmed metastatic triple-negative breast carcinoma; repeat IHC showed ER-, PR-, HER2 IHC 1+ (not amplified), GATA3+, CK7+. PD-L1 CPS on the metastatic specimen was 8 (below threshold for pembro rechallenge). NGS on the metastatic biopsy demonstrated the expected BRCA1 p.E1250* and TP53 p.R248W, with acquisition of PIK3CA p.H1047R and a new CDK4 amplification not seen on the primary tumor.

She received palliative radiation to the right 5th posterior rib (20 Gy in 5 fractions) with excellent pain response (5/10 down to 1/10). Olaparib 300 mg BID was initiated for first-line metastatic TNBC given germline BRCA1 mutation, along with zoledronic acid 4 mg IV every 4 weeks for bone-modifying therapy.

At her 3-month restaging (approximately cycle 4-5 of olaparib), CT showed the hepatic lesions had decreased -- the 1.2 cm lesion to 0.9 cm, the 0.8 cm to 0.6 cm -- and osseous sites were stable without new disease. She was continued on olaparib at full dose.

Today she presents for her 6-month on-therapy visit. She reports she is tolerating olaparib fairly well overall. She notes ongoing mild fatigue (grade 1, present most afternoons, does not prevent her from working part-time), and mild nausea intermittently, particularly in the morning shortly after taking her olaparib dose. She has been managing the nausea with ondansetron 4 mg PRN, which she uses 2-3 times per week. She reports taking olaparib consistently with food per instruction, which has helped somewhat. She denies vomiting. No diarrhea, constipation, or abdominal pain. She reports her rib pain is well-controlled, currently rated 0-1/10, using acetaminophen 1000 mg TID scheduled; she has not needed opioid breakthrough medication for approximately 6 weeks. She denies new bone pain. She denies dyspnea, cough, or chest pain. She reports stable mild tingling in her feet bilaterally, unchanged from prior (residual grade 1 neuropathy from prior paclitaxel), not worsening. She denies headache, visual changes, or new neurologic symptoms. No skin rash.

Importantly, she reports that her sister who was referred for BRCA1 testing returned a positive result for the same pathogenic variant. She is understandably supportive of her sister and somewhat emotionally burdened by this news. She has spoken with gynecologic oncology regarding risk-reducing bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy (RRSO) for herself and reports she is ready to proceed; a date is being coordinated.

She remains on levothyroxine 50 mcg daily (pembrolizumab-associated hypothyroidism, well-controlled; last TSH normal).

She is currently not working full-time but is doing 3 days per week of part-time teaching, which she values highly for her sense of purpose and routine.

DIAGNOSTIC BILATERAL MAMMOGRAPHY AND TARGETED ULTRASOUND OF THE LEFT BREAST AND LEFT AXILLA

CLINICAL INDICATION:
38-year-old premenopausal woman, nulliparous, presenting with a self-detected left breast mass noticed approximately 6 weeks ago. No prior mammographic history on file at this institution. Family history of maternal grandmother with breast cancer (age 60) and maternal aunt with ovarian cancer (age 52). Referred for diagnostic workup of palpable left breast abnormality.

------------------------------------------------------------
SCAN TYPE / TECHNIQUE:
Diagnostic bilateral digital mammography (craniocaudal and mediolateral oblique projections bilaterally, with additional spot compression and magnification views of the left upper outer quadrant). Targeted grayscale and Doppler ultrasound of the left breast and left axilla performed immediately following mammography with a 12 MHz linear transducer.

------------------------------------------------------------
FINDINGS:

MAMMOGRAPHY:

Left Breast:
There is a 3.1 cm irregular, high-density mass centered in the upper outer quadrant of the left breast, located at approximately the 10 o'clock position. The mass demonstrates markedly spiculated margins with radiating dense strands extending into the adjacent parenchyma. Associated fine pleomorphic and linear microcalcifications are present within and immediately surrounding the mass, extending over a span of approximately 3.5 cm. Architectural distortion is noted in the immediate vicinity of the dominant lesion. The overlying skin is smooth and of normal thickness. The nipple is not retracted. No additional suspicious lesions are identified elsewhere in the left breast.

Axillary tail (left): Asymmetric density noted in the left axillary tail region, correlating with the sonographic finding described below.

Right Breast:
The right breast is composed of scattered fibroglandular tissue (ACR category B). No dominant mass, architectural distortion, or suspicious calcifications are identified in the right breast. No axillary adenopathy is visualized on the right MLO projection.

Breast Density: Heterogeneously dense breast tissue bilaterally (ACR category C), which may reduce the sensitivity of mammography.

------------------------------------------------------------
ULTRASOUND -- LEFT BREAST:

A hypoechoic, irregularly marginated mass is identified at the 10 o'clock position of the left breast, 3 cm from the nipple, corresponding to the mammographic finding. The mass measures 3.1 x 2.8 x 2.5 cm (transverse x AP x craniocaudal). Margins are angulated to spiculated. Internal echotexture is heterogeneous with areas of mild posterior acoustic enhancement. The mass demonstrates mild internal vascularity on color Doppler interrogation. Posterior acoustic enhancement is present. No definite calcification shadowing is resolved on ultrasound. There is mild thickening of the Cooper's ligaments in the immediate vicinity of the lesion. No distinct satellite nodules are identified in the adjacent parenchyma on real-time survey.

Skin and subcutaneous tissues: No skin thickening or subcutaneous edema.
Nipple-areolar complex: Normal. No subareolar ductal dilation.

------------------------------------------------------------
ULTRASOUND -- LEFT AXILLA:

Survey of the left axilla demonstrates a single morphologically abnormal lymph node at level I. This node measures 2.0 x 1.3 cm in maximal dimensions. The fatty hilum is markedly attenuated and essentially absent. The cortex is eccentrically thickened, measuring up to 9 mm at its greatest extent. Color Doppler shows displaced and disordered hilar vascularity. No additional discrete level I-II lymph nodes are identified with convincingly abnormal morphology, though background reactive-appearing small nodes are noted. The level III/infraclavicular region was not surveyed on the current examination.

------------------------------------------------------------
ULTRASOUND -- RIGHT AXILLA:

No morphologically abnormal lymph nodes identified in the right axilla. Several small nodes with preserved fatty hila are seen, consistent with normal reactive lymph nodes.

------------------------------------------------------------
BIRADS ASSESSMENT:

Left breast mass at 10 o'clock: ACR BI-RADS Category 5 -- Highly suggestive of malignancy.
Left axillary lymph node: Morphologically abnormal; findings highly suspicious for nodal involvement.
Right breast: ACR BI-RADS Category 1 -- Negative.

------------------------------------------------------------
IMPRESSION:

1. There is a 3.1 cm spiculated, high-density left upper outer quadrant mass at the 10 o'clock position with associated pleomorphic microcalcifications, highly suspicious for primary breast malignancy. Ultrasound characterizes this as an irregular, hypoechoic solid mass with internal vascularity.

2. A single morphologically abnormal left axillary lymph node measuring 2.0 cm with near-complete loss of the fatty hilum and eccentric cortical thickening is identified, raising strong concern for metastatic nodal involvement.

3. No additional suspicious lesions are identified in either breast.

4. No skin thickening, nipple involvement, or subcutaneous edema.

5. The right breast and right axilla appear unremarkable.

Findings were discussed with the referring clinician at the time of interpretation. Tissue sampling of the left breast mass and the abnormal left axillary lymph node is warranted for histopathologic diagnosis.

Restaging Evaluation -- Metastatic Triple-Negative Breast Cancer

CLINICAL INDICATION:
38-year-old woman with metastatic triple-negative breast cancer (primary diagnosis ~18 months prior), germline BRCA1 mutation carrier, status post neoadjuvant Keytruda/ddAC/pacli, left lumpectomy with ALND, adjuvant WBRT with regional nodal irradiation, and adjuvant cape/Keytruda. Distant recurrence confirmed by biopsy approximately 3 months ago involving bone (right 5th rib, T10 vertebral body, right iliac wing) and liver (two right lobe lesions). She subsequently received palliative radiation to the right 5th posterior rib and was initiated on ola (Lynparza) 300 mg twice daily with concurrent zoledronic acid. This examination is for restaging and response assessment after approximately 3 months of Lynparza therapy. Comparison: CT chest/abdomen/pelvis and bone scan performed at the time of metastatic diagnosis (prior study).

TECHNIQUE:
CT chest, abdomen, and pelvis performed with intravenous iohexol contrast (100 mL), administered in portal venous phase. Multiplanar reformations obtained. Whole-body bone scintigraphy performed with 25 mCi Tc-99m MDP intravenously administered; planar images obtained at 3 hours post-injection. SPECT/CT of the thorax and pelvis performed for lesion characterization.

---

FINDINGS:

CT CHEST:
Lungs: The lungs are clear. No pulmonary nodules, masses, consolidation, or ground-glass opacities. No pleural effusion. No pneumothorax.
Mediastinum: Heart size within normal limits. No pericardial effusion. No mediastinal or hilar lymphadenopathy by size criteria. Aorta and great vessels appear unremarkable.
Chest wall/ribs: Post-radiation changes are noted at the right 5th posterior rib with surrounding soft tissue density consistent with treatment effect. No new rib lesions identified on CT. No cortical disruption beyond the previously irradiated site. No pathologic fracture.
Left breast/axilla: Expected post-lumpectomy and post-radiation changes in the left upper outer quadrant with architectural distortion, skin thickening, and metallic clip artifact at the surgical bed. No new suspicious soft tissue mass. No axillary, supraclavicular, or internal mammary lymph node enlargement.

CT ABDOMEN:
Liver: Two hypoattenuating lesions in the right hepatic lobe, previously identified, are again seen. The larger lesion in segment VII previously measured 1.2 cm and now measures approximately 0.9 cm in greatest diameter -- a modest decrease in size. The smaller lesion in segment VI previously measured 0.8 cm and now measures approximately 0.6 cm. Both lesions remain hypoattenuating on portal venous phase with no arterial hyperenhancement or washout pattern to suggest a new hepatocellular process. No new hepatic lesions are identified. The liver parenchyma is otherwise homogeneous in attenuation. No biliary ductal dilatation. Portal and hepatic veins are patent.
Gallbladder: Unremarkable. No cholelithiasis.
Pancreas: Normal in morphology and attenuation. No ductal dilatation, peripancreatic fat stranding, or masses.
Spleen: Mildly prominent, measuring 12.1 cm in craniocaudal dimension (previously 11.6 cm), without discrete focal lesions. Mild splenomegaly may reflect ola-related myelosuppression effect vs. physiologic variation; no discrete splenic lesions.

Kidneys: Both kidneys appear normal with no masses, hydronephrosis, or perinephric abnormality. No renal calculi.
Bowel: Small and large bowel loops are unremarkable without wall thickening, obstruction, or free air.
Lymph nodes: No retroperitoneal, mesenteric, or para-aortic lymphadenopathy. No celiac or porta hepatis adenopathy.
Ascites: None.

CT PELVIS:
Right iliac wing: The lytic lesion in the right iliac wing previously measured 1.8 cm. On this examination it measures approximately 1.7 cm with similar extent of cortical disruption and no new periosteal reaction. No pathologic fracture identified. Overall appearance is similar to the prior study with no interval growth.
Bladder: Normal without wall thickening or intraluminal filling defect.
Uterus and adnexa: Uterus is anteverted and of normal size. No adnexal masses or pelvic free fluid. No pelvic lymphadenopathy.
Bones (pelvis): No additional lytic, sclerotic, or expansile osseous lesions in the pelvis beyond the known right iliac wing lesion.

SPINE (CT characterization of bone scan findings):
T10 vertebral body: The previously noted focus of concern at T10 is again identified with mild trabecular coarsening. There is no associated soft tissue mass or epidural extension. Vertebral body height is maintained.

WHOLE-BODY BONE SCINTIGRAPHY WITH SPECT/CT:
Right 5th posterior rib: Residual mild uptake at the right 5th posterior rib, significantly diminished compared to the prior study, in keeping with post-radiation treatment effect and resolving bone injury. No extension of activity into adjacent ribs.
T10 vertebral body: Stable low-level increased uptake at T10. SPECT/CT correlation demonstrates this localizes to the vertebral body without extension to the posterior elements or spinal canal. No new adjacent vertebral involvement.
Right iliac wing: Stable focus of mildly increased uptake in the right iliac wing corresponding to the known lytic lesion. Activity appears similar in intensity to the prior scan without obvious progression.
Remainder of skeleton: No new foci of abnormal increased radiotracer uptake. The skull base, shoulders, sternum, contralateral ribs, thoracic and lumbar spine (other than T10), sacrum, and bilateral lower extremity long bones are unremarkable.

---

IMPRESSION:

1. The two right hepatic lobe lesions have both decreased in size compared to the prior examination (segment VII: 1.2 cm to 0.9 cm; segment VI: 0.8 cm to 0.6 cm), consistent with favorable hepatic response in the setting of ongoing Lynparza therapy. No new liver lesions.

2. Known osseous sites of disease -- T10 vertebral body, right iliac wing, and post-radiation right 5th rib -- are stable without convincing interval growth or development of new skeletal lesions. The right 5th rib shows diminished radiotracer activity on bone scan compatible with treatment and radiation response.

3. No new pulmonary nodules, pleural disease, or lymphadenopathy. No new osseous lesions.

4. Mild splenic enlargement, new compared to prior, is nonspecific; in this clinical context may relate to ongoing marrow-suppressive therapy, though a baseline assessment is recommended.

5. Overall, findings are consistent with favorable response at the hepatic sites and stability of known osseous disease -- no evidence of new or progressive metastatic involvement on this study.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
Ms. Okonkwo is a 39-year-old (turning 40) West African woman with a history of stage IIB left breast TNBC (diagnosed approximately 2 years prior), now with confirmed metastatic recurrence to bone (right 5th posterior rib, T10 vertebral body, right iliac wing) and liver (two right lobe lesions), diagnosed approximately 18 months after her definitive surgery. She presents today for follow-up at approximately 6 months of systemic therapy with olaparib 300 mg BID, initiated as first-line treatment for metastatic disease in the context of a confirmed germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant (c.3750G>A, p.E1250*).

Brief disease and treatment timeline for context: She originally received neoadjuvant pembrolizumab (Keytruda) plus dose-dense AC (ddAC x4 cycles) followed by weekly paclitaxel x12 weeks with concurrent pembro, then underwent left lumpectomy with sentinel lymph node biopsy and ALND. Surgical pathology demonstrated residual IDC 1.4 cm (ypT1c ypN0(i+), RCB-II, Miller-Payne grade 3). She completed adjuvant WBRT with regional nodal irradiation, then adjuvant capecitabine (Xeloda) x6 cycles (dose-reduced to 750 mg/m2 BID for HFS) with pembrolizumab maintenance to complete 17 total pembro cycles. She developed pembrolizumab-associated hypothyroidism during adjuvant therapy, currently managed with levothyroxine 50 mcg daily. Approximately 18 months post-surgery, she presented with right rib pain and was found to have multifocal osseous metastases and two small hepatic lesions on imaging, confirmed pathologically as metastatic TNBC (iliac wing biopsy: poorly differentiated carcinoma, ER/PR/HER2 negative, GATA3+, CK7+). NGS on the metastatic biopsy revealed the original driver alterations (TP53 p.R248W, BRCA1 p.E1250*, PTEN loss, RB1 p.Q670*) plus acquired PIK3CA p.H1047R and CDK4 amplification. PD-L1 CPS was 8 on the metastatic specimen. She received palliative RT to the symptomatic right 5th rib (20 Gy/5 fx) prior to starting olaparib. Zoledronic acid (Zometa) 4 mg IV q4 weeks was also initiated concurrently.

At her 3-month restaging scan, she had a partial response in the liver (1.2 cm lesion decreased to 0.9 cm, 0.8 cm lesion decreased to 0.6 cm) with stable osseous disease. She is now presenting for the 6-month follow-up visit.

Today's visit: The patient reports overall tolerating olaparib reasonably well. She endorses persistent mild fatigue, which she rates as 3-4/10 in severity -- present since starting olaparib and consistent throughout, not worsening. She takes ondansetron (Zofran) 4 mg PRN with good effect for occasional mild nausea, predominantly in the mornings; she estimates she takes it approximately 3-4 times per week. She denies vomiting. No dyspnea, chest pain, or palpitations. She continues to have grade 1 peripheral sensory neuropathy in both feet (numbness, tingling), which she reports is stable and unchanged from prior assessments; she is still on duloxetine 60 mg daily. She reports that her right rib pain is well-controlled (1/10) since completing palliative radiation -- she has not required her breakthrough opioid for several weeks. She denies new bone pain. No headaches or neurologic symptoms to suggest CNS involvement. She is currently working part-time as a high school biology teacher, having reduced her schedule due to fatigue. She reports her mood is "okay," with some understandable anxiety about disease trajectory. She has spoken with gynecologic oncology about risk-reducing bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy (RRSO) given her germline BRCA1 mutation, and she has decided to proceed; a surgical date has been scheduled.

Her sister recently tested positive for the BRCA1 familial variant and has been referred for genetic risk management.

METHODOLOGY
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
DNA was extracted from FFPE-embedded tumor tissue following manual macrodissection to enrich for tumor cellularity. A hybrid-capture approach targeting all coding exons of 324 cancer-relevant genes was performed, encompassing oncogenes, tumor suppressor genes, and genes of clinical actionability in solid tumors. The panel detects single-nucleotide variants (SNVs), small insertions/deletions (indels), copy number alterations (amplifications and homozygous deletions), and gene rearrangements/fusions. Tumor mutational burden (TMB) and microsatellite instability (MSI) are calculated genome-wide using an in silico normalization method. Homologous recombination deficiency (HRD) scoring incorporates genomic loss of heterozygosity (gLOH), large-scale state transitions (LST), and telomeric allelic imbalance (TAI). RNA-based fusion detection was performed from the same FFPE block using an anchored multiplex PCR approach targeting 55 known fusion gene partners. PD-L1 CPS was performed by immunohistochemistry (Dako 22C3 pharmDx assay) on an adjacent section and reported independently; result incorporated herein. A note has been appended regarding reflexed germline testing.

GERMLINE VARIANT NOTE
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
The BRCA1 p.E1250* (c.3750G>A) variant was identified at a VAF of 51% -- a VAF in the range expected for a heterozygous germline variant in a tumor specimen with significant somatic LOH. The allele frequency, combined with the copy number loss at the BRCA1 locus (17q12-q21), is consistent with biallelic inactivation via germline mutation plus somatic loss of heterozygosity (LOH), a classical two-hit mechanism. This pattern strongly suggests a germline origin. Reflexed germline confirmatory testing was performed from a separate peripheral blood sample. Germline result: PATHOGENIC GERMLINE VARIANT CONFIRMED -- BRCA1 c.3750G>A (p.E1250*, NM_007294.4), heterozygous, classified as Pathogenic (ClinVar ID: associated). A separate, complete germline report has been issued and communicated directly to the ordering provider and genetic counseling service.

IMPLICATIONS: Patient is at significantly elevated lifetime risk for breast cancer (contralateral) and ovarian cancer. Cascade testing of first-degree relatives strongly recommended. PARP inhibitor therapy (e.g., olaparib, talazoparib) may be applicable per current guidelines for germline BRCA1/2-mutated breast cancer.

INTERPRETATION AND MOLECULAR SUMMARY
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
This triple-negative invasive ductal carcinoma (grade 3, stage IIB) harbors a genomic profile consistent with the basal-like / BRCAness molecular subtype, with several clinically significant findings:

1. BIALLELIC BRCA1 INACTIVATION (germline p.E1250* + somatic LOH): This is the most therapeutically relevant finding. BRCA1 loss of function results in profound homologous recombination deficiency (HRD score 54, positive), rendering the tumor sensitive to PARP inhibitors (olaparib, talazoparib) and platinum-based chemotherapy. The germline nature of this variant has critical implications for the patient and her biological relatives.

2. TP53 p.R248W (gain-of-function hotspot): This well-characterized dominant-negative p53 mutation is present at high VAF (48%), consistent with a clonally dominant alteration arising early in tumor evolution. The R248W variant not only abolishes normal p53 transcriptional activity but confers oncogenic gain-of-function properties (enhanced invasion, chemotherapy resistance in some contexts). This alteration is nearly universal in TNBC/basal-like tumors and does not currently have a direct targeted therapeutic option, though ongoing trials with APR-246 (eprenetapopt) and related p53-reactivating agents are in development.

3. PTEN HOMOZYGOUS DELETION: Complete loss of PTEN results in unopposed PI3K/AKT/mTOR pathway activation. In the absence of a PIK3CA-activating mutation (which is absent here, as expected in BRCA1-mutant TNBC), PTEN loss is the primary driver of PI3K pathway dysregulation. This may confer relative resistance to some chemotherapy agents and is a potential future target for PI3K pathway inhibition (alpelisib, capivasertib, ipatasertib).

4. RB1 TRUNCATING MUTATION (p.Q670*): Inactivation of RB1 further destabilizes cell cycle control in a tumor already lacking functional p53. Biologically, combined TP53 and RB1 loss is a hallmark of highly aggressive TNBC and is associated with cisplatin sensitivity in some preclinical models. Notably, RB1 loss renders the tumor intrinsically resistant to CDK4/6 inhibitors (palbociclib, ribociclib, abemaciclib), which is consistent with the ER-negative receptor status and would preclude use of these agents in any future hormone receptor conversion scenario.

5. MYC AMPLIFICATION (copy number ~14): High-level MYC amplification is a recurrent event in TNBC and is associated with high Ki-67 (here 85%) and aggressive clinical behavior. MYC-amplified tumors may be sensitized to Aurora A kinase inhibitors or BET bromodomain inhibitors (investigational). This finding is prognostic but not currently directly actionable with approved therapies.

6. CDKN2A p.P114L (subclonal, VAF 22%): This subclonal variant is of uncertain significance. CDKN2A encodes p16INK4A (a CDK4/6 inhibitor) and p14ARF (a p53 stabilizer). Given pre-existing RB1 inactivation and TP53 mutation, the functional contribution of this CDKN2A variant is likely redundant with respect to cell cycle dysregulation.

7. HRD SIGNATURE: The HRD score of 54, combined with a dominant SBS3 mutational signature (34% of mutations), is entirely consistent with BRCA1-mediated homologous recombination deficiency. This signature validates the functional consequences of BRCA1 biallelic loss at the genomic level. The APOBEC mutational signatures (SBS2/13, combined ~33%) are frequently co-observed in TNBC and may contribute to mutational heterogeneity.

8. PD-L1 CPS 15 (POSITIVE): PD-L1 expression above the threshold of 10 supports eligibility for pembrolizumab in combination with chemotherapy in the early-stage/neoadjuvant and potentially metastatic settings, per KEYNOTE-522 and KEYNOTE-355 data.

MOLECULAR DIAGNOSIS: Triple-negative invasive ductal carcinoma, basal-like genomic subtype, with germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant (biallelic inactivation), TP53 gain-of-function mutation, PTEN homozygous deletion, RB1 truncation, MYC amplification, and positive HRD. PD-L1 CPS 15. MSS. TMB 9.2 mut/Mb (not TMB-high).

Patient: Marlena Okonkwo
Referring Service: Breast Surgery / Primary Care
Reason for Consultation: New diagnosis of left breast invasive ductal carcinoma, TNBC, Stage IIB -- evaluation and treatment planning

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT

Ms. Okonkwo is a 38-year-old nulliparous premenopausal woman of West African descent referred for oncology consultation following new diagnosis of triple-negative breast cancer of the left breast. She presents today accompanied by her partner for treatment planning discussion.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Ms. Okonkwo is a 38-year-old woman who first noticed a firmness in her left breast approximately 6 weeks ago while performing self-breast examination. She described it as a hard, non-tender lump in the upper outer area of the left breast. She sought evaluation through her primary care provider, who confirmed a palpable mass and expedited referral to breast surgery. Diagnostic bilateral mammogram demonstrated a 3.1 cm irregular, spiculated, high-density mass in the left upper outer quadrant with associated microcalcifications, BI-RADS 5. Subsequent ultrasound confirmed a 3.1 x 2.8 x 2.5 cm hypoechoic irregular mass at the 10 o'clock position, 3 cm from the nipple. A single enlarged left axillary lymph node (2.0 cm, loss of fatty hilum) was also identified.

Core needle biopsy of the breast mass was performed, confirming invasive ductal carcinoma, grade 3 (Nottingham 9/9), ER-negative (Allred 0/8), PR-negative (Allred 0/8), HER2 1+ by IHC (negative). Ki-67 proliferative index 85%. Lymphovascular invasion was present. Stromal TILs noted at 40%. Fine-needle aspiration of the ipsilateral axillary lymph node confirmed metastatic carcinoma consistent with breast primary. PD-L1 CPS was measured at 15 by the 22C3 assay.

Staging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast and bone scan showed no evidence of distant metastatic disease. Imaging consistent with locoregional disease only -- no pulmonary nodules, no liver lesions, no osseous lesions on bone scan.

Comprehensive genomic profiling (324-gene panel) on the core biopsy specimen was performed and is notable for: TP53 p.R248W (pathogenic), BRCA1 p.E1250* (pathogenic, VAF 51% -- germline confirmed on reflex testing), PTEN homozygous deletion, RB1 p.Q670*, MYC amplification (copy number 14), CDKN2A p.P114L. Microsatellite stable, TMB 9.2 mut/Mb, HRD score 54 (positive). Germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant has been confirmed on reflexed germline testing and reported separately to genetic counseling; patient has been informed.

The case was reviewed at multidisciplinary tumor board today. Consensus: Stage IIB (T2 N1 M0) TNBC with BRCA1 germline mutation; given tumor size, node-positive disease, and aggressive biology, neoadjuvant systemic therapy is the preferred approach to allow for tumor downstaging, assessment of pathologic response, and potential breast-conserving surgery.

Ms. Okonkwo denies fevers, chills, weight loss, night sweats, dyspnea, cough, bone pain, or neurological symptoms. No nipple discharge, skin changes, or palpable right breast lesion. She reports mild anxiety regarding the diagnosis but otherwise feels well and is currently working full-time as a high school biology teacher.
